reasoning: 30% ABV. Pure coca leaf distillate (alkaloids removed) + 36 botanicals: green tea, African mint, guarana, yerba mate, ginseng, lavender, juniper, bitter orange. Cool/fresh coca-leaf-and-mint character is defining — high cooling, moderate herbal, no honey.

40% ABV, 27 plants/spices, beetroot spirit base + honey + saffron + spices. The honey signature is defining — sweetest of the classical herbal liqueurs, warm spice register. Pairs with whiskey (Vieux Carré, Bobby Burns, Singapore Sling).

Provençal thyme liqueur (1898), 40% ABV. Wild thyme harvested in May + sage + lemon verbena + angelica. Single-herb-led with supporting botanicals — herbal-forward, low everything else, mild thyme cooling.
55% ABV, 130 herbs since 1737, secret monk recipe. Defining herbal liqueur — maximum herbal, dry rather than sweet, no honey/anise, signature menthol-cooling finish from peppermint and alpine herbs.

Tobacco liqueur (Ted Breaux/Jade/Lucid absinthe), 31% ABV, Louisiana Perique tobacco. Described as 'cigar-box and dried-leaf rather than smoke — dry, spicy modifier. Best used in tiny amounts.' Not really herbal — more spice-tobacco. Low everything else; spice and herbal are the carriers.

German digestif since 1934, 35% ABV, 56 herbs: citrus, licorice, anise, ginger, juniper, ginseng. Herbal-anise-spice-forward sweetened liqueur. Sits between amaro and herbal liqueur; placed here since it lacks the bitter-defining character of amaro proper.
Traditional French absinthe (68% ABV) with 9 herbs including Grande Wormwood. Complex herbal-anise with wormwood bitterness. Distinct from pastis (sweet, simpler).

Spanish vanilla-citrus liqueur (Cuarenta y Tres), 31% ABV, 43 botanicals — but the dominant flavors are vanilla and citrus, not herbal. Sits more in the dessert-liqueur space; lower herbal than other entries.

Piedmontese mountain-pine liqueur from Quaglia (1890), 35% ABV. Pine resin + cones + mountain-pine buds cold-infused and distilled. Producer notes: 'clean, resinous, lightly spicy conifer profile.' Pine = signature cooling axis.

French pastis — anise + licorice + Provençal herbs, 45% ABV typical. Sweeter and less complex than absinthe; no wormwood. Used as a substitute when an absinthe-style flavor is wanted without the bitter intensity.

Italian herbal liqueur since 1860, 40% ABV, ~70 botanicals including saffron (yellow color). Anise-forward sweetened herbal — distinctive from Chartreuse by the anise punch and the saffron-driven yellow palate.
Italian olive leaves harvested in spring + lemon finishing. 38% ABV. Savory/herbal, NOT sweet, NO honey/anise/spice/cooling. A unique near-savory herbal liqueur.
40% ABV, same 130 botanicals as Green but honey-sweetened, lower-proof, mellower. Tasting notes: honey, saffron, anise. The honey-mellowed version of Green — less cooling, more honeyed.
